Psalm 140 is a prayer born from real danger. The psalmist is not describing abstract evil or theological wickedness—he is surrounded by people who actively scheme against him. They sharpen their tongues like snakes, spread snares along his path, and hide traps waiting for him to stumble. This is visceral, immediate threat. Yet notice what he does not do: he does not rely on his own cunning or strength to escape. Instead, he cries out to the LORD, and in that cry, he makes a declaration that cuts through the panic: "You are my God." In the midst of hostile plotting, the psalmist anchors himself to the one relationship that matters. God is his God—not because circumstances prove it, but because he chooses to believe it when belief is most costly.

The psalmist's prayers for justice are not expressions of petty vengeance. He asks that God's justice be done, that evil schemes fail, and that those who traffic in violence and lies face the consequences of their own words. When he prays that burning coals fall upon his enemies and that calamity hunts down the violent, he is calling on God to be the just judge He claims to be. This is the hard part of trusting God: it means trusting His judgment even when we are tempted to take matters into our own hands, even when justice seems slow in coming. The psalmist does not execute his own revenge. He brings his fear and his anger to God and leaves the verdict with Him.

What sustains the psalmist through this ordeal is his confidence in a fundamental truth: the LORD upholds justice for the poor and defends the cause of the needy. He knows this not because his current circumstances are comfortable, but because he knows God's character. And that knowledge gives him certainty about the future—not that he will escape unscathed, but that righteousness ultimately belongs with God. Those who are righteous will praise His name and dwell in His presence. This is not a promise that danger will disappear or that enemies will suddenly love you. It is the promise that God's justice will finally be vindicated, and those who trust Him will be vindicated with it.

When you pray this psalm, you are invited to do what the psalmist did: speak your fear honestly to God, name the real threats and real enemies you face, and then return to what you know to be true about Him. His strength shields us. His justice will be done. And our future is not determined by whether our enemies succeed today, but by whether we belong to the God who always triumphs in the end. That is where your confidence must rest.
